P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Dear Senator Schmitz: 
Executive/Legal

March 26, 1982 
Enfarcement 
322-6441 
Re: 
Advice Letter 
No. A-82-052 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your letter of March 22, 1982, regarding 
use of your legislative district office after hours for 
political activities. 
As I understand your question, you 
would like to use an automatic typewriter, leased by your 
campaign, in your district office after normal working hours. 
You will be using supplies purchased by your campaign, and 
will utilize volunteer campaign workers. 
The only State 
resources which would be used in connection with this activity 
would be the utilities (e.g. electricity, heat) and office 
space. 
The fair market value of the use of these facilities 
would, in all probability, be de minimis, and thus would not 
be itemizable by the committee-Under the Political Reform 
Act, and would not impose any reporting obligations on the 
Legislature. 
Although this limited use of your district office may 
not be reportable under the Political Reform Act, there may 
still be a question of whether any use of state facilities 
for political purposes is ever permissible. 
The California 
Supreme Court addressed this issue in the case of Stanson v. 
Mott (1976) 17 Cal. 3d 206. 
If you want to know whether the 
rule in that case applies to your situation, I suggest you 
consult the Attorney General. 
For your information, I am enclosing a copy of the 
Commission's existing regulations dealing with reporting the 


Senator John G. Schmitz 
March 26, 1982 
Page Two 
use of State resources for political purposes. 
2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Sections 18420 and 18423.

sections.question: Whether a State Senator may use an automatic typewriter leased by his campaign in his legislative district office after normal working hours for political campaign purposes, using campaign-purchased supplies and volunteer campaign workers, when the only State resources used would be utilities and office space.
sections.conclusion: The fair market value of the use of State facilities (utilities and office space) would, in all probability, be de minimis and thus would not be itemizable or impose reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. However, the FPPC notes there may still be a broader constitutional question regarding whether any use of state facilities for political purposes is permissible, and directs the Senator to consult the Attorney General regarding the rule in Stanson v. Mott (1976).
sections.question_synthetic: A State Senator seeks clarification on whether he can use his campaign-leased typewriter and supplies in his state legislative district office after hours for campaign activities, when state utilities and office space would be the only state resources involved.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The limited use of state utilities and office space is likely de minimis and not reportable under the Political Reform Act, but the FPPC defers to the Attorney General on the broader constitutional question of whether state facilities may be used for political purposes at all.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (Senator Schmitz's March 22 letter) and FPPC response (March 26 advice letter). Extracted content from FPPC response letter as primary document. Clear question and conclusion sections present. Minor OCR artifacts in Senator's letterhead ('o DISTRICT OFFICii:' and some garbled text) do not materially affect extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a State Senator may use an automatic typewriter leased by his campaign in his legislative district office after normal working hours for political campaign purposes, using campaign-purchased supplies and volunteer campaign workers, when the only State resources used would be utilities and office space.

CONCLUSION: The fair market value of the use of State facilities (utilities and office space) would, in all probability, be de minimis and thus would not be itemizable or impose reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. However, the FPPC notes there may still be a broader constitutional question regarding whether any use of state facilities for political purposes is permissible, and directs the Senator to consult the Attorney General regarding the rule in Stanson v. Mott (1976).

As I understand your question, you would like to use an automatic typewriter, leased by your campaign, in your district office after normal working hours. You will be using supplies purchased by your campaign, and will utilize volunteer campaign workers. The only State resources which would be used in connection with this activity would be the utilities (e.g. electricity, heat) and office space. The fair market value of the use of these facilities would, in all probability, be de minimis, and thus would not be itemizable by the committee-Under the Political Reform Act, and would not impose any reporting obligations on the Legislature. Although this limited use of your district office may not be reportable under the Political Reform Act, there may still be a question of whether any use of state facilities for political purposes is ever permissible. The California Supreme Court addressed this issue in the case of Stanson v. Mott (1976) 17 Cal. 3d 206. If you want to know whether the rule in that case applies to your situation, I suggest you consult the Attorney General. For your information, I am enclosing a copy of the Commission's existing regulations dealing with reporting the Senator John G. embedding.summary: The FPPC advises State Senator Schmitz that his proposed after-hours use of a campaign-leased typewriter in his legislative district office would not trigger reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act because state resources involved are de minimis, but recommends consulting the Attorney General on constitutional constraints under Stanson v. Mott.
